Gold that melts under no ordinary fire can be undone by the heat of enough tongues speaking at once. Reputation is strong but not invulnerable, and the force of collective voice is one of the few things that can soften what was hard. Be careful what you contribute to the chorus.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 火 Fire 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Guo Yu 國語·周語下 (Zhōu Yǔ, Zhou Speeches)

Read the full reading
377

良liáng藥yào苦kǔ口kǒu，忠zhōng言yán逆nì耳ěr

liáng yào kǔ kǒu zhōng yán nì ěr

good medicine bitter to mouth, loyal words harsh to ear

Good medicine tastes bitter; honest advice is hard to hear.

The body resists the remedy even as it needs it, and the ear flinches from the word that could save it. What costs us nothing to receive usually costs us nothing in value. The friend who tells you what you do not want to hear is offering something the comfortable ones cannot.

諺語 Proverb Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 金 Metal 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Shi Ji 史記·留侯世家 (Liú Hóu biography)

Read the full reading
378

同tóng甘gān共gòng苦kǔ

tóng gān gòng kǔ

share the sweet, share the bitter

True companions go through the good times and the bad together; loyalty means staying for both.

Anyone can show up for the celebration. The question is always who shows up for the hard part. Those people — the ones who stayed when it was bitter — are the ones whose presence at the sweet moments actually means something.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 土 Earth 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Shi Ji 史記, Yue Yi biography (樂毅列傳); Sima Qian

Read the full reading
379

忠zhōng心xīn耿gěng耿gěng

zhōng xīn gěng gěng

loyal heart bright bright

Wholehearted loyalty; steadfast and sincere devotion.

The bright star does not debate whether to shine on cloudy nights. Loyalty does not perform itself only when witnesses are present; its quality is precisely that it continues without the audience. What is constant without prompting is the only kind of constancy worth trusting.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 火 Fire 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Traditional Chinese idiom (common in historical and literary contexts)

Read the full reading
380

患huàn難nán與yǔ共gòng

huàn nán yǔ gòng

hardship and difficulty together share

Face hardships and difficulties together; stand by each other in adversity.

The canoe that fills in the rapids is no time to find out whether your companion knows how to bail. True alliance is confirmed not in the harbor but in the storm, when the question of who stays is answered without being asked. That is the kind of knowing that cannot be taught.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 水 Water 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Shi Ji 史記·刺客列傳 (Assassins biography section)

Read the full reading
381

知zhī音yīn難nán覓mì

zhī yīn nán mì

one who understands the music is hard to find

True friends who understand the depths of one's soul are rare and precious.

Zhong Ziqi could hear in Bo Ya's playing what he was really playing — the mountains when he was playing mountains, the flowing water when he was playing flowing water — and when Ziqi died, Bo Ya broke his strings. There was no longer anyone who could hear. Such listeners are rare; such recognition is rarer. Those who truly hear you are worth everything.

諺語 Proverb Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 水 Water 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Shi Ji 史記 / Liezi 列子·湯問 (Tāng Wèn, story of Bo Ya and Zhong Ziqi)

Read the full reading
382

送sòng君jūn千qiān里lǐ，終zhōng須xū一yī別bié

sòng jūn qiān lǐ zhōng xū yī bié

escort you a thousand li, must eventually part

No matter how far you accompany someone, there must eventually be a parting; all goodbyes are inevitable.

The road of accompaniment is long and then it ends, no matter how many thousand li were traveled together. The parting is not a failure of the companionship but its natural end, and what was given along the thousand li does not depart with the person. What love deposited in the journey is not returned with the farewell.

諺語 Proverb Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 金 Metal 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in literary and social contexts)

Read the full reading
383

風fēng雨yǔ同tóng舟zhōu

fēng yǔ tóng zhōu

wind rain same boat

In the same boat through wind and rain; sharing hardships together.

The storm does not ask who paid more for their passage. In the same boat, all hands bail, all voices cry warning, and the shared urgency dissolves the usual distinctions of rank. Those who have weathered the same storm together carry a knowledge of each other that fair weather never produces.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 水 Water 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Sun Zi 孫子·九地 (Jiǔ Dì, Nine Terrains)

Read the full reading
384

同tóng舟zhōu共gòng濟jì

tóng zhōu gòng jì

same boat, cross together

People in the same difficult situation must work together.

When the boat is small and the water is wide, no one can afford the luxury of not helping. The shared danger is the great equalizer, stripping away difference to reveal the only fact that matters: we are in this together, and together is the only direction that leads to shore. Crisis, at least, offers this gift.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 水 Water 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Sun Zi 孫子·九地 (Jiǔ Dì, Nine Terrains)

Read the full reading
385

心xīn有yǒu靈líng犀xī一yī點diǎn通tōng

xīn yǒu líng xī yī diǎn tōng

heart has spiritual rhinoceros horn, one point connected

Two people with a deep connection understand each other without words; a meeting of minds.

The rhinoceros horn was said to carry a vein of spirit from base to tip, and two horns when placed together could transmit understanding through that shared channel. There are people whose minds meet yours in exactly this way: at a single point of contact, the whole meaning passes through, wordlessly and completely. This is what recognition feels like.

詩詞 Verse Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 火 Fire 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Li Shangyin 李商隱·《無題》 (Wú Tí, Untitled poem, Tang Dynasty)

Read the full reading
386

感gǎn恩ēn圖tú報bào

gǎn ēn tú bào

feel gratitude, plan to repay

Feel grateful and plan to return the favor; respond to kindness with appropriate reciprocity.

Gratitude without the intention to return is merely pleasant feeling, and pleasant feeling without action eventually dissipates. The plan to repay is not obligation but direction, a way of keeping the gratitude alive by giving it somewhere to go. What is received gracefully becomes part of the cycle that keeps goodwill moving through the world.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 水 Water 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Traditional Chinese idiom (common in social and moral contexts)

Read the full reading
387

成chéng人rén之zhī美měi

chéng rén zhī měi

help complete another's goodness

Help others achieve their goals and realize their good qualities; facilitate others' success.

The gardener who helps the adjacent tree reach more sunlight does not thereby diminish their own light. Enabling another person's best outcome is a form of the highest sociability, one that understands abundance to be expandable rather than fixed. The generous one who makes others' beauty possible is also participating in it.

成語 Adage Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 木 Wood 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·顏淵 (Yán Yuān XII)

Read the full reading
388

贈zèng人rén玫méi瑰guī，手shǒu有yǒu餘yú香xiāng

zèng rén méi guī shǒu yǒu yú xiāng

give person roses, hand retains lingering fragrance

When you give to others, you also receive; generosity leaves something behind in the giver.

The hand that gives the rose does not return empty. The fragrance is not a byproduct but a second gift, the one the giver did not anticipate. What passes through your hands on its way to someone else leaves a trace that the mere holding of it does not. Generosity is the only form of spending that leaves the spender richer.

諺語 Proverb Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 木 Wood 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying

Read the full reading
389

雪xuě中zhōng送sòng炭tàn

xuě zhōng sòng tàn

in snow send charcoal

Provide timely help in times of need; send charcoal in a snowstorm.

The charcoal given in the blizzard is worth ten times the same charcoal given on a pleasant day. What is given at the exact moment of need demonstrates not merely generosity but attentiveness, the kind that notices what is needed and moves before being asked. This is the highest form of a gift: the one that arrives before the request.

諺語 Proverb Friendship, Trust & Speech Present-minded 火 Fire 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Song Dynasty 宋·范成大詩 (Fàn Chéngdà poem usage)

Read the full reading
390

冰bīng凍dòng三sān尺chǐ，非fēi一yī日rì之zhī寒hán

bīng dòng sān chǐ, fēi yī rì zhī hán

three feet of ice is not the cold of a single day

Nothing deep — a skill, a habit, a ruin — forms overnight; it is the slow sum of many unremarkable days.

The ice that can hold your weight was not laid down last night; it took a hundred quiet cold hours you never counted. Whatever has grown thick in you, for good or for ill, was built one ordinary day at a time, so watch the days.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Looks back 水 Water 鼠 Year of the Rat

Source 《論衡·狀留》 by 王充 (Wang Chong, Han dynasty)

Read the full reading
391

心xīn急jí吃chī不bù了liǎo熱rè豆dòu腐fu

xīn jí chī bù liǎo rè dòu fu

anxious heart cannot eat hot tofu

Impatience will not speed things up; haste ruins what needs time.

The tofu steaming from the pot does not care how hungry you are; it will burn your mouth at exactly the same temperature regardless. What requires cooling will cool in its own time, and the person who tries to eat before it is ready learns nothing useful except the texture of regret. Patience is often just knowing the actual timeline of things.

俗語 Saying Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 火 Fire 鼠 Year of the Rat

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (suyu)

Read the full reading
392

克kè己jǐ復fù禮lǐ

kè jǐ fù lǐ

subdue the self, return to propriety

Master your own impulses and align your conduct with what is right; virtue begins as self-restraint.

The hardest thing you will ever govern is already sitting inside your own ribs. Rein the wanting self back to the line of what is fitting, and notice that the discipline nobody sees is the only kind that ever changes you.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source 《論語·顏淵》 (Analects 12.1), Confucius to Yan Yuan

Read the full reading
393

大dà巧qiǎo若ruò拙zhuō

dà qiǎo ruò zhuō

great skill appears as clumsiness

True mastery often looks effortless and plain; the greatest craftspeople appear to do less, not more.

The mark of someone who has done something ten thousand times is that they look like they are hardly doing it at all. The effort has gone underground. What remains on the surface is just the work — clean, unhurried, and without any announcement of how much it cost.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經, ch. 45 (Laozi)

Read the full reading
394

大dà智zhì若ruò愚yú

dà zhì ruò yú

great wisdom seems like foolishness

The deepest intelligence looks unremarkable from outside — the truly wise feel no need to appear clever, so their brilliance reads as plainness.

The sharpest person in the room is often the quietest, letting others mistake restraint for slowness. You don't have to win every exchange to be right; there is a kind of power that only works by looking like it isn't trying.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Su Shi 蘇軾, letter to Ouyang Xiu 賀歐陽少師致仕啟 (Song dynasty)

Read the full reading
395

寬kuān以yǐ待dài人rén，嚴yán以yǐ律lǜ己jǐ

kuān yǐ dài rén yán yǐ lǜ jǐ

lenient in dealing with people, strict in disciplining oneself

Be generous with others, strict with oneself.

The ruler used on yourself should be made of iron; the one held toward others should bend a little. To demand of yourself what you forgive in others is not hypocrisy reversed but the original form of fairness. It changes both parties, quietly.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Song Dynasty 宋·朱熹《論語集注》 (Lúnyǔ Jí Zhù, Commentary on the Analects)

Read the full reading
396

小xiǎo不bù忍rěn則zé亂luàn大dà謀móu

xiǎo bù rěn zé luàn dà móu

small cannot endure then disrupts great plan

Inability to endure small irritations will ruin great plans.

The grain of sand in the mechanism is small and the mechanism is intricate and one leads to the destruction of the other. Patience with the minor irritation is not passive but protective, a guard set against the small things that would displace the large work. What you cannot hold lightly, you cannot hold.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·衛靈公 (Wèi Líng Gōng XV)

Read the full reading
397

欲yù善shàn其qí身shēn，然rán後hòu能néng施shī於yú人rén

yù shàn qí shēn rán hòu néng shī yú rén

wish to improve the self, only then able to give to others

Cultivate yourself first before extending goodness to others.

The lamp cannot light another room until it has itself gathered enough flame. This is not selfishness but sequence: the capacity to give is built through the work of becoming, and that work cannot be skipped by going directly to the giving. Tend to yourself so that your tending has something to offer.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Da Xue 大學 (Dà Xué, The Great Learning)

Read the full reading
398

泰tài山shān不bù讓ràng土tǔ壤rǎng

tài shān bù ràng tǔ rǎng

Mount Tai does not refuse even a grain of soil

True greatness accepts and incorporates everything, even the smallest contribution; be inclusive.

Mount Tai is not too proud for a single grain of sand, and this is precisely why it has become what it is. Greatness is not about the dramatic exclusion of the small but the patient acceptance of every contribution, knowing that the mountain is the sum of the ordinary and the extraordinary held together without distinction.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Li Si 李斯·《諫逐客書》 (Jiàn Zhú Kè Shū, Remonstrance Against Expelling Guests, Qin Dynasty)

Read the full reading
399

知zhī人rén者zhě智zhì，自zì知zhī者zhě明míng

zhī rén zhě zhì zì zhī zhě míng

knowing others is wisdom; knowing oneself is enlightenment

True intelligence is understanding others; true wisdom is understanding yourself.

It is easy to read a room. It takes longer to read yourself in the room. The person who can do both moves through the world without constant correction — they already know what needs adjusting before anyone has to tell them.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經, ch. 33 (Laozi)

Read the full reading
400

金jīn無wú足zú金jīn，人rén無wú完wán人rén

jīn wú zú jīn rén wú wán rén

gold has no pure gold, person has no perfect person

No gold is perfectly pure, no person is perfectly flawless; perfection is unattainable.

The jeweler who rejects all gold less than one hundred percent pure will have no gold and no jewelry. Imperfection is not a flaw in the design; it is the design. What we ask of gold and of people is not perfect purity but genuine value, and the two are different requirements entirely.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
401

強qiáng中zhōng自zì有yǒu強qiáng中zhōng手shǒu

qiáng zhōng zì yǒu qiáng zhōng shǒu

among the strong there are naturally even stronger hands

No matter how strong you are, there is always someone stronger; there is always a higher level.

The strongest person in any room has arrived at the top of that particular room. Outside the room, the ranking changes. This is the correct relationship between pride and humility: genuine strength is well-earned and real, and also fully aware that it is local, contextual, and temporary. The best competitors know this best.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in martial arts culture)

Read the full reading
402

知zhī己jǐ知zhī彼bǐ，百bǎi戰zhàn不bù殆dài

zhī jǐ zhī bǐ, bǎi zhàn bù dài

know yourself, know the other, and in a hundred battles you are never in peril

Understanding both your own nature and the situation lets you face any contest safely.

You study your opponent hard and study yourself hardly at all, which is why the same defeat keeps finding you. The blind spot is never across the table, it is behind your own eyes. Know the shape of your own flinch and no contest can surprise you into it.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Looks ahead 金 Metal 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source The Art of War 孫子兵法

Read the full reading
403

知zhī止zhǐ不bù殆dài

zhī zhǐ bù dài

know stopping, not in danger

Knowing when to stop keeps one out of danger; the wisdom of knowing one's limits.

The one who does not know where to stop will eventually overstep, and overstepping has its own gravity. To know the edge and choose not to approach it is not timidity but the exercise of a particular kind of wisdom: the map that includes the limit is more useful than the one that imagines the territory is unlimited. Know where enough is.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經·第四十四章 (Chapter 44)

Read the full reading
404

謙qiān謙qiān君jūn子zǐ

qiān qiān jūn zǐ

the humble, humble noble person

One who adds humility to humility — a person of standing who stays modest — moves through the world with grace and meets good fortune.

You have watched someone with every reason to boast simply not bother, and found it magnetic. Rank that lowers its own voice is the rarest kind; the deeper the water, the less noise it makes going by.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source I Ching 易經, Hexagram 15 Qian 謙

Read the full reading
405

內nèi聖shèng外wài王wáng

nèi shèng wài wáng

inner sage, outer king

Cultivate inner virtue to govern outward; internal sagely wisdom expressed as external leadership.

The governance of the world begins with the governance of the self, and the outer kingdom is only as stable as the inner one. To lead others while the interior is in disorder is to build a structure whose foundation depends on the luck of those who don't look too closely at the ground floor. Sage inwardly first.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Zhuangzi 莊子·天下 (Tiān Xià, Under Heaven chapter)

Read the full reading
406

君jūn子zǐ之zhī過guò，如rú日rì月yuè之zhī食shí

jūn zǐ zhī guò rú rì yuè zhī shí

the noble person's fault, like an eclipse of the sun or moon

A noble person's faults are like eclipses — visible to all, but they acknowledge and correct them openly.

The eclipse is visible to everyone watching the sky, and the noble person does not hide behind a cloud. When the error is made, it is made in the same light as everything else, acknowledged without concealment, and the sky afterward is the same sky. The fault admitted completely becomes the eclipse that passes, and what was obscured briefly is bright again.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·子張 (Zǐ Zhāng XIX)

Read the full reading
407

天tiān外wài有yǒu天tiān，人rén外wài有yǒu人rén

tiān wài yǒu tiān rén wài yǒu rén

beyond sky there is sky, beyond person there is person

There is always someone more capable; do not become arrogant.

Summit after summit reveals another summit, and the horizon retreats at the same pace you approach it. This is not discouraging but orienting: there is always more to learn, always someone whose skill exceeds yours. Pride is simply forgetting how large the sky is.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
408

天tiān行xíng健jiàn，君jūn子zǐ以yǐ自zì強qiáng不bù息xī

tiān xíng jiàn, jūn zǐ yǐ zì qiáng bù xī

Heaven moves with vigor; the noble person accordingly strengthens the self without cease

The cosmos never rests in its turning, so a worthy person keeps improving without pause.

The sky does not take a night off from turning, and it asks nothing of you but the same. Let the ceaseless wheel overhead set your pace: not frantic, just unstopping — the kind of effort that forgets how to quit.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Looks ahead 金 Metal 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source 《周易·象傳》 (I Ching, Xiang commentary on the Qian hexagram)

Read the full reading
409

山shān外wài有yǒu山shān，樓lóu外wài有yǒu樓lóu

shān wài yǒu shān lóu wài yǒu lóu

beyond the mountain there is mountain, beyond the tower there is tower

There is always something greater; the world extends beyond any visible limit.

Every summit commands a view of the next summit, and the tower that seems tallest from below reveals another tower when you finally reach it. This is not frustration but orientation: the world is larger than the current position can see, and that largeness is not a rebuke to where you are but an invitation to keep going. More always exists.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying

Read the full reading
410

海hǎi納nà百bǎi川chuān

hǎi nà bǎi chuān

sea accepts a hundred rivers

The sea accepts all rivers; have an inclusive and broad-minded nature.

The sea has no opinion about which river it receives — the muddy or the clear, the great or the small — and this indifference is not emptiness but capacity. What can receive everything without losing its nature is not nothing but everything, concentrated. Breadth of spirit is measured not by what it will take in but by what it will not exclude.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Lin Zexu 林則徐 couplet (Qing Dynasty statesman)

Read the full reading
411

人rén各gè有yǒu志zhì

rén gè yǒu zhì

each person has their own aspiration

Every person has their own goals and ideals; respect the diversity of human purpose.

The field of human aspiration is not a single crop but a garden of different species, each with its own requirements and its own season and its own fruit. What one person is growing has no obligation to resemble what another is growing, and the gardener wise enough to recognize this wastes no time trying to make the plum tree bear rice.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Shi Ji 史記 (common sentiment in classical literature)

Read the full reading
412

修xiū心xīn養yǎng性xìng

xiū xīn yǎng xìng

cultivate heart, nourish nature

Cultivate the inner mind and nurture one's true nature; the work of inner development.

The mind left uncultivated fills with whatever passes through it, useful and useless alike, and the nature left unnourished grows wild in the direction of whatever pulls hardest. To tend to both is the continuous work of becoming someone you would choose to be. There is no finished version of this work; it is always in progress, always worthwhile.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Taoist and Confucian cultivation tradition

Read the full reading
413

內nèi外wài兼jiān修xiū

nèi wài jiān xiū

inside and outside both cultivate

Cultivate both inner virtue and outer capability; develop internally and externally.

The building whose exterior is magnificent and interior is hollow will not stand inspection from anyone who enters. Inner cultivation without outer expression remains private; outer achievement without inner grounding remains fragile. The one who tends both lives in a home that holds under all kinds of weather.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Traditional Chinese philosophical and martial arts principle

Read the full reading
414

功gōng成chéng身shēn退tuì

gōng chéng shēn tuì

achievement complete, self withdraws

After success, gracefully step back; do not cling to position after accomplishing the task.

The greatest compliment to the work is to let it stand on its own once it is finished. The one who stays past the completion of their purpose becomes a different kind of presence, one that diminishes rather than honors what was built. To step back at the right moment is not diminishment but the final, perfect stroke.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Looks ahead 金 Metal 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經·第九章 (Chapter 9)

Read the full reading
415

寧níng靜jìng致zhì遠yuǎn

níng jìng zhì yuǎn

through tranquility, reach the far

Only a calm, undistracted mind accomplishes far-reaching aims.

You cannot see far from a shaking place. Quiet the room and quiet the mind, and the horizon you were straining toward comes into view on its own.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Looks ahead 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Zhuge Liang 諸葛亮, 誡子書

Read the full reading
416

無wú欲yù則zé剛gāng

wú yù zé gāng

no desire then strong

Without desire, one cannot be manipulated; freedom from desire produces strength.

The one who wants nothing cannot be bribed with anything. Desire is the handle by which a person is controlled, and the one who has released the handle stands where no lever can reach. This is not cold withdrawal from life but a different kind of warmth: the freedom of not needing what others can withhold.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Lin Zexu 林則徐 couplet (Qing Dynasty statesman)

Read the full reading
417

知zhī止zhǐ而ér後hòu有yǒu定dìng

zhī zhǐ ér hòu yǒu dìng

knowing stopping, then having stability

Only by knowing when to stop can one achieve genuine stability and stillness.

The river cannot rest while it is moving, but the lake that has found its basin is still in a way the river cannot be. Knowing where to stop is not resignation but the finding of the basin, the place where the motion that has been appropriate becomes the stillness that is also appropriate. Stability comes to those who know when the moving is done.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Da Xue 大學 (Dà Xué, The Great Learning)

Read the full reading
418

窮qióng則zé獨dú善shàn其qí身shēn

qióng zé dú shàn qí shēn

in poverty then cultivate only oneself

When one cannot influence the world, focus on cultivating oneself; retreat to inner work.

When the avenue to wide influence closes, the cultivation of the self remains open. This is not defeat but reorientation: the world that cannot be improved from the outside can still be improved from within, and what is improved within eventually finds its way back out again, in a different season. The inner work is never wasted.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Mencius 孟子·盡心上 (Jìn Xīn I)

Read the full reading
419

欲yù速sù則zé不bù達dá

yù sù zé bù dá

desire speed, then you do not arrive

Rushing defeats its own purpose.

The part of you that wants this finished by tomorrow is the same part that will make you redo it next week. Speed feels like progress and often it is only noise. Slow the hand, and watch how much less you have to walk back.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 火 Fire 馬 Year of the Horse

Source Analects 論語, Book 13

Read the full reading
420

人rén非fēi聖shèng人rén，孰shú能néng無wú過guò

rén fēi shèng rén shú néng wú guò

persons are not sages, who can be without fault

No one is perfect; everyone makes mistakes — what matters is whether you learn from them.

The standard of perfection is not the relevant standard. The relevant standard is whether the error, once made, was acknowledged and addressed or protected and excused. To be human is to err; the variable is in the response. What is done with the mistake is the actual measure.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Zuozhuan 左傳·宣公二年 (Xuān Gōng Year 2)

Read the full reading
421

借jiè人rén之zhī長cháng，補bǔ己jǐ之zhī短duǎn

jiè rén zhī cháng bǔ jǐ zhī duǎn

borrow others' strengths, fill in one's own shortfalls

Learn from others' strengths to compensate for your own weaknesses.

The structure that knows where it is weak and finds complementary strength nearby becomes the structure that stands. Pride insists on hiding the gap; wisdom finds someone whose surplus fills it, and the exchange strengthens both. We are all more complete in the company of what we lack.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
422

知zhī足zú者zhě常cháng樂lè

zhī zú zhě cháng lè

those who know sufficiency are always happy

The person who knows when they have enough will always find contentment; contentment is the real wealth.

The accumulation never ends if you don't decide it has. More becomes the water table: always lower than where you are standing. But the person who draws the line — who says this is enough — suddenly has everything, because enough is the one quantity that actually satisfies.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經, ch. 46 (Laozi); common folk form

Read the full reading
423

知zhī足zú者zhě樂lè

zhī zú zhě lè

those who know sufficiency are joyful

Those who know when they have enough are truly happy; contentment is the source of joy.

Joy is not a reward for having enough but a result of recognizing that what you have is enough. The recognition is the thing, not the quantity. What looks like poverty from outside can be experienced as plenitude from within when the one inside has learned the art of the satisfied gaze. This is not resignation but arrival.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經·第三十三章 (Chapter 33)

Read the full reading
424

勝shèng人rén者zhě有yǒu力lì，自zì勝shèng者zhě強qiáng

shèng rén zhě yǒu lì, zì shèng zhě qiáng

who overcomes others has force; who overcomes himself is strong

Beating other people only takes power; mastering yourself is the rarer, truer strength.

Anyone with enough force can win over another person. The one victory that actually costs you something is the one with no opponent in the room but you, and that is the only fight that leaves you stronger than you started.

詩詞 Verse Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 猴 Year of the Monkey

Source 《老子·道德經》 chapter 33 (Tao Te Ching)

Read the full reading
425

尺chǐ有yǒu所suǒ短duǎn，寸cùn有yǒu所suǒ長cháng

chǐ yǒu suǒ duǎn, cùn yǒu suǒ cháng

a foot can fall short; an inch can be long enough

Even the larger measure has its shortcomings and the smaller its strengths — everyone has gifts and gaps, so judge no one, and least of all yourself, by a single scale.

The thing you envy in someone else comes bundled with a lack you can't see, and the small measure you dismiss in yourself is exactly long enough for a job the tall ruler can't do. Stop ranking whole people by the one axis you happened to notice.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 猴 Year of the Monkey

Source Chu Ci 楚辭, Bu Ju 卜居 (attrib. Qu Yuan)

Read the full reading
426

班bān門mén弄nòng斧fǔ

bān mén nòng fǔ

show off axe in front of master carpenter's door

To show off one's skill in front of an expert; arrogant display of limited ability.

The apprentice who demonstrates his three cuts to the master who has made ten thousand has confused enthusiasm with competence. There is a season for showing what you know, and it usually comes after you have learned when to be quiet in the presence of someone who knows more. The axe is real; the audience is not impressed.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 猴 Year of the Monkey

Source Ming Dynasty 明·梅之渙《題李白墓》 (Tí Lǐ Bái Mù, inscription at Li Bai's tomb)

Read the full reading
427

邯hán鄲dān學xué步bù

hán dān xué bù

Handan learn walk

Imitate others foolishly, losing one's own style; blind imitation leads to losing one's original self.

The young man went to learn the elegant walk of Handan and studied so hard to copy it that he forgot his own natural gait. He returned unable to walk at all, having traded something real for something that was never his to take. Imitation is a tool for learning, not a destination.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 猴 Year of the Monkey

Source Zhuangzi 莊子·秋水 (Qiū Shuǐ, Autumn Floods)

Read the full reading
428

寧nìng缺quē毋wú濫làn

nìng quē wú làn

rather lack than be excessive

Better to have less of quality than more of inferior; do not fill a gap with something substandard.

An empty chair at the table is honest about what it holds. A chair filled with the wrong presence is full of a different kind of emptiness, one that pretends. What is absent can always be found; what is wrongly present must first be removed before anything better can arrive.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Traditional Chinese proverb (common in folk and cultural contexts)

Read the full reading
429

知zhī恥chǐ近jìn乎hū勇yǒng

zhī chǐ jìn hū yǒng

knowing shame is near to courage

The capacity to feel shame is close to courage; awareness of one's failures is the first step to overcoming them.

The one who cannot feel shame also cannot improve, having removed the mechanism that registers the gap between what they are and what they could be. To feel shame clearly, without drowning in it, and then to act on it is the nearest thing to courage available in the ordinary life. The acknowledgment is already most of the bravery.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 火 Fire 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Li Ji 禮記·中庸 (Zhōng Yōng, Doctrine of the Mean)

Read the full reading
430

莫mò道dào君jūn行xíng早zǎo，更gèng有yǒu早zǎo行xíng人rén

mò dào jūn xíng zǎo gèng yǒu zǎo xíng rén

don't say you left early, there are others who left even earlier

Don't boast about being first; there is always someone who was there before you.

The road at dawn is never as empty as it looks from behind. Those who rise earliest often discover that someone was already there before them, already climbing, already at work, and the discovery adjusts the boast into perspective. The world is full of early risers who say nothing about it.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
431

驕jiāo傲ào使shǐ人rén落luò後hòu，虛xū心xīn使shǐ人rén進jìn步bù

jiāo ào shǐ rén luò hòu xū xīn shǐ rén jìn bù

pride makes person fall behind, humble heart makes person progress

Pride leads to falling behind; humility leads to advancement.

The proud vessel is already full and so cannot receive. The empty cup can take in everything offered. What looks like disadvantage in the humble person — the openness, the willingness to be taught — is exactly the capacity that fills them while the proud stand still. The cup that holds more grows more.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Mao Zedong 毛澤東 speech usage; roots in classical humility tradition

Read the full reading
432

問wèn心xīn無wú愧kuì

wèn xīn wú kuì

ask the heart, no shame

Act so that when you examine your own conscience, you find nothing to be ashamed of.

Before any external judge arrives, there is always the inner one. If that one can look at what you did and find nothing to hide from, the external verdict matters far less. A clear conscience is the only kind of ease that cannot be taken away.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Common Chinese idiom; Qing dynasty novel usage

Read the full reading
433

無wú過guò而ér能néng改gǎi，善shàn莫mò大dà焉yān

wú guò ér néng gǎi shàn mò dà yān

if having fault and can correct, no greater virtue

If you have made an error and correct it, there is no greater virtue than this.

The correction of the mistake is worth more than its never having occurred, because the one who corrects has done two things: acknowledged the truth and moved toward it. The person who has never erred is just the person whose errors have not been visible yet. The one who corrects them visibly has demonstrated something rarer.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 金 Metal 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Zuozhuan 左傳·宣公二年 (Xuān Gōng Year 2)

Read the full reading
434

見jiàn賢xián思sī齊qí

jiàn xián sī qí

see the worthy, think to equal them

When you see someone virtuous, aspire to be like them.

The worthy person is not a source of envy but of aspiration, a signal of what is possible rather than a reminder of what is lacking. The instinct to equal rather than to diminish is the one that grows. What is excellent in another is a map drawn in flesh and behavior, showing that the destination is real and reachable.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 木 Wood 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·里仁 (Lǐ Rén IV)

Read the full reading
435

過guò而ér不bù改gǎi，是shì謂wèi過guò矣yǐ

guò ér bù gǎi shì wèi guò yǐ

fault without correcting, this is called fault

A fault not corrected is the true fault; the real mistake is failing to address the error.

The error itself is the first act; failing to address it is the second. The second is the one that defines the person, because it is chosen where the first was often not. To make a mistake and know it and then not correct it is to commit to a path whose eventual destination is visible from the beginning. The fault is in the continuation, not only the origin.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·衛靈公 (Wèi Líng Gōng XV)

Read the full reading
436

洗xǐ心xīn革gé面miàn

xǐ xīn gé miàn

wash the heart, reform the face

Thoroughly reform oneself; make a fresh start with a clean conscience.

The face turned outward changes last, but the change must begin in the invisible room of the heart where no one else is present. What is washed inward eventually shows on the surface, and no amount of surface adjustment can substitute for the deeper work. Reformation moves from the center outward, always.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Yi Jing 易經·繫辭上 (Xì Cí I)

Read the full reading
437

滿mǎn招zhāo損sǔn，謙qiān受shòu益yì

mǎn zhāo sǔn, qiān shòu yì

fullness invites loss; humility receives gain

Complacency and self-satisfaction erode what you have; staying humble and open is what keeps you growing — this, the ancients said, is the way of Heaven.

The moment you decide you've arrived is the moment the ground starts sliding back. Stay a little unfinished, a little hungry to learn, and the room keeps making space for you — the cup that thinks it is full stops being poured into.

諺語 Proverb Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Book of Documents 尚書, Counsels of the Great Yu 大禹謨

Read the full reading
438

知zhī足zú常cháng樂lè

zhī zú cháng lè

knowing sufficiency, one is always at ease

Contentment with what you have brings lasting happiness.

You keep moving the finish line the moment you reach it, so the running never once turns into arriving. The ease you are chasing is not on the far side of the next thing, it is in noticing you already have enough for tonight. Set the want down and feel how quiet the room gets.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經, ch. 46

Read the full reading
439

知zhī足zú知zhī止zhǐ

zhī zú zhī zhǐ

know sufficiency, know stopping

Know when you have enough and when to stop; contentment and restraint.

The hand that reaches past enough does not gain more; it loses the contentment that was already there. Knowing how to stop is the rarer skill, the one that keeps fortune from becoming its own misfortune. Those who know where to rest their foot rarely step into the void.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 水 Water 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Tao Te Ching 道德經·第四十四章 (Chapter 44)

Read the full reading
440

虛xū懷huái若ruò谷gǔ

xū huái ruò gǔ

a mind as open and empty as a valley

True humility keeps the mind hollow like a valley — open enough to receive every stream, every view, without needing to be right.

The most secure people you know are strangely easy to correct — they hold their opinions loosely, like a valley holding water it never grips. Empty a little of your certainty and notice how much more flows in.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Dao De Jing 道德經, chapter 15 (Laozi)

Read the full reading
441

量liàng力lì而ér行xíng

liàng lì ér xíng

measure strength then act

Act within one's actual capabilities; know your limits and work within them.

The bridge that knows its weight limit does not refuse the traveler; it simply tells the truth about what it can bear. Self-knowledge of capacity is not timidity but precision, and the person who calibrates carefully before moving rarely collapses mid-span. Honest accounting of one's own strength is the beginning of reliable action.

成語 Adage Humility & Self-Mastery Present-minded 土 Earth 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Zuozhuan 左傳·隱公十一年 (Yǐn Gōng Year 11)

Read the full reading
442

世shì態tài炎yán涼liáng

shì tài yán liáng

world's manner hot and cold

The ways of the world are warm when you prosper and cold when you fall; human relationships follow fortune.

The same people who filled the room when you were successful find themselves suddenly busy when your fortunes turn. This is not cynicism but information about the nature of circumstance-based affection. Those who remain when the room empties are the only ones who were there in the first place.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 火 Fire 鼠 Year of the Rat

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in literature and folk contexts)

Read the full reading
443

人rén有yǒu志zhì氣qì不bù怕pà窮qióng

rén yǒu zhì qì bù pà qióng

person with determination not afraid of poverty

A person with determination is not afraid of poverty; spirit overcomes material lack.

Poverty cannot take what it never owned: the direction of the will, the clarity of the purpose, the knowledge of what the person is working toward. The body can be impoverished while the interior life is productive and rich and entirely unconstricted. The person with genuine aspiration keeps building in conditions that would stop the one whose will was borrowed.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 火 Fire 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb

Read the full reading
444

冬dōng天tiān來lái了le，春chūn天tiān還hái會huì遠yuǎn嗎ma

dōng tiān lái le chūn tiān hái huì yuǎn ma

winter has come, can spring be far

After hardship, good times are coming; if winter is here, spring cannot be far away.

The cold is complete and the ground is hard and the trees are bare, and none of this means the year has stopped. Winter is not an ending but the fullest form of one season, and what follows it has never once failed to come. This knowledge belongs to the earth itself; we borrow it when the dark is heavy.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 水 Water 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Inspired by Shelley's Ode to the West Wind; deeply embedded in Chinese folk expression

Read the full reading
445

富fù貴guì不bù能néng淫yín，貧pín賤jiàn不bù能néng移yí

fù guì bù néng yín pín jiàn bù néng yí

wealth and honor cannot corrupt, poverty and low station cannot change

Wealth cannot corrupt, poverty cannot sway; the noble person's character is unchanging.

The measure of character is taken in both directions: the person who is not corrupted by wealth and the person who is not broken by poverty is the same person, holding the same center through opposite conditions. This is not rigidity but rootedness, the quality of a trunk that gives in neither direction despite the varying pressure of seasons.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 金 Metal 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Mencius 孟子·滕文公下 (Téng Wén Gōng II)

Read the full reading
446

苦kǔ盡jìn甘gān來lái

kǔ jìn gān lái

bitterness exhausted, sweetness arrives

After hardship comes happiness; endure the bitter and the sweet will follow.

There is a particular quality to sweetness that only arrives after you have spent some time without it. The relief of spring tastes different after a long winter. The meal after the fast has a depth that ordinary meals cannot reach.

成語 Adage Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 火 Fire 牛 Year of the Ox

Source Common Chinese proverb; recorded in 元曲 Yuan dynasty drama

Read the full reading
447

吃chī得dé苦kǔ中zhōng苦kǔ，方fāng為wéi人rén上shàng人rén

chī dé kǔ zhōng kǔ fāng wéi rén shàng rén

able to eat the bitterest of bitter, only then become a person above persons

Only those who endure the greatest hardships rise above others.

The hardship that is willingly consumed — not endured passively but taken in deliberately as part of the work — is the substance of which exceptional lives are made. This is not celebration of suffering for its own sake but recognition of what difficulty does when it is met with discipline rather than avoidance: it builds something in the person that no comfortable life can build, and that thing becomes the foundation of everything afterward.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 木 Wood 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (common in pedagogy and martial arts culture)

Read the full reading
448

山shān重chóng水shuǐ複fù疑yí無wú路lù

shān chóng shuǐ fù yí wú lù

mountains fold upon mountains, waters double back, suspect no road

When things seem utterly hopeless and no way forward appears — the prelude to unexpected hope.

The path that seems to dead-end against a mountain has not always ended; sometimes the mountain simply has not yet shown you the pass. Despair at the fold of the road is premature cartography, drawn too early in the journey. Hold on a little longer and let the map correct itself.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 水 Water 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Lu You 陸遊·《遊山西村》 (Yóu Shān Xī Cūn, Song Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
449

梅méi花huā香xiāng自zì苦kǔ寒hán來lái

méi huā xiāng zì kǔ hán lái

plum blossom fragrance comes from bitter cold

The plum's fragrance comes from enduring bitter cold; strength and beauty are forged through hardship.

The fragrance that stops passersby in winter was bought by exactly the cold that everyone else was fleeing. What the plum makes of frost is not complaint but blossom, and what it offers to the frozen air is everything the summer flowers cannot, because summer asks nothing. The sweetest things are those that survived the season that should have killed them.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 水 Water 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Wang Mian 王冕·《墨梅》 (Mò Méi, Ink Plum poem, Yuan Dynasty)

Read the full reading
450

生shēng於yú憂yōu患huàn，死sǐ於yú安ān樂lè

shēng yú yōu huàn sǐ yú ān lè

life born from worry and hardship, death from peace and pleasure

Adversity is what creates vitality; excessive comfort leads to decay.

The muscle that is never challenged wastes. The character that is never tested softens. And the nation or person that has arranged their world to remove all friction finds that what they removed was also what was keeping them alert. Some degree of difficulty is not the obstacle to flourishing; it is one of the conditions for it.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 水 Water 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Mencius 孟子·告子下 (Gào Zǐ II)

Read the full reading
451

真zhēn金jīn不bù怕pà火huǒ煉liàn

zhēn jīn bù pà huǒ liàn

true gold does not fear fire smelting

What is genuine has nothing to fear from testing; true quality survives any challenge.

The gold enters the furnace and the fire does its work and when the gold exits it is more purely itself than before. What is real welcomes the test because the test confirms what was already true. Fear of scrutiny is the first signal that something in the claim might not hold under heat.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 火 Fire 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
452

若ruò非fēi一yī番fān寒hán徹chè骨gǔ，哪nǎ得dé梅méi花huā撲pū鼻bí香xiāng

ruò fēi yī fān hán chè gǔ nǎ dé méi huā pū bí xiāng

if not for the cold piercing to the bone, how could the plum blossom's fragrance reach the nose

Without enduring extreme hardship, one cannot achieve true accomplishment; the fragrance is earned by the frost.

The fragrance that stops you on a January morning was not made by the warmth; it was made by the cold that the plum survived without retreating. The blossom owes its power to the exact severity of what it endured. What is achieved without difficulty has a different quality from what required everything, and the nose knows.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 水 Water 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Huang Baishan 黃蘗禪師·《上堂開示頌》 (Tang Dynasty Chan Buddhist poem)

Read the full reading
453

草cǎo木mù皆jiē兵bīng

cǎo mù jiē bīng

grass and trees all soldiers

See enemies in everything; a state of extreme fear where harmless things appear threatening.

The defeated army sees enemy soldiers in every rustling leaf, every wind-bent stem. This is not cowardice but what fear does to the perceptual apparatus: it promotes everything to the level of threat. To know when one's alarm system has exceeded its brief is the beginning of recovery from terror.

成語 Adage Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 木 Wood 虎 Year of the Tiger

Source Jin Shu 晉書·謝安傳 (Xiè Ān biography, Battle of Feishui)

Read the full reading
454

柳liǔ暗àn花huā明míng

liǔ àn huā míng

willows dark, flowers bright

After darkness comes light; when things seem hopeless, a new way opens.

The path that closes into shadow has not ended; it has only not yet shown you what lies past the bend. The willows crowd in and the light seems to stop, and then without announcement the world opens into blossoms again. This is the shape of almost every story that turned out well.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 木 Wood 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Lu You 陸游·《遊山西村》 (Yóu Shān Xī Cūn, Song Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
455

人rén間jiān正zhèng道dào是shì滄cāng桑sāng

rén jiān zhèng dào shì cāng sāng

the righteous way in the human world is vicissitude

The true path of humanity is one of constant change and transformation; the right road runs through upheaval.

The road that is right does not run through comfort alone. Vicissitude — the rise and fall, the building and losing — is the actual texture of the forward path, and those who expected the right road to be smooth have confused rightness with ease. The same sea that becomes a mulberry field becomes a sea again. This is not failure but the righteous way.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Mao Zedong 毛澤東·《七律·人民解放軍占領南京》 (1949 poem)

Read the full reading
456

天tiān將jiāng降jiàng大dà任rèn於yú斯sī人rén也yě，必bì先xiān苦kǔ其qí心xīn志zhì

tiān jiāng jiàng dà rèn yú sī rén yě, bì xiān kǔ qí xīn zhì

when Heaven is about to lay a great charge on a person, it first embitters their heart and will

Great responsibility is preceded by suffering that hardens the mind; the ordeal is the training, not the punishment.

The weight pressing on you is not a verdict, it is a fitting. Heaven does not hand the large task to the untested heart, so read the bitterness as measurement — something is deciding you can carry more than you think.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source 《孟子·告子下》 (Mencius, Gaozi II)

Read the full reading
457

平píng地dì起qǐ風fēng波bō

píng dì qǐ fēng bō

flat ground raises wind and waves

Trouble arising from nowhere; unexpected disturbance in calm conditions.

The calm was real and then it was not, and no map predicted this. Flat ground is no guarantee and still water is no promise; some turbulences are internal before they are visible. The one who stays ready in the calm is not paranoid but simply realistic about the nature of any surface.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 水 Water 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb

Read the full reading
458

一yī朝zhāo被bèi蛇shé咬yǎo，十shí年nián怕pà草cǎo繩shéng

yī zhāo bèi shé yǎo shí nián pà cǎo shéng

once bitten by snake, ten years afraid of grass rope

A bad experience leaves lasting fear; once bitten, twice shy.

The grass rope and the snake share only their shape, but the memory of pain does not distinguish between them. Trauma rewrites the nervous system's reading of the world, and the coiled rope at dusk looks exactly like what it does not need to look like. Understanding this is not weakness but the beginning of patience with one's own alarm systems.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Looks back 木 Wood 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
459

久jiǔ病bìng成chéng良liáng醫yī

jiǔ bìng chéng liáng yī

long illness becomes good doctor

Those who suffer from a condition long enough become expert in it.

The body that has been educated by years of illness has been taught by the most thorough teacher available. The knowledge is unwanted but it is complete, lived into from the inside, and the understanding of the condition that emerges cannot be replicated by study alone. Sometimes depth of experience substitutes for degree.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 土 Earth 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
460

窮qióng則zé變biàn，變biàn則zé通tōng，通tōng則zé久jiǔ

qióng zé biàn biàn zé tōng tōng zé jiǔ

exhausted then change, changed then open, open then lasting

When you reach an impasse, change; change leads to flow; flow leads to longevity.

The river that hits a cliff does not stop but finds another way, and this is not defeat but intelligence. Exhaustion is the teacher that arrives when all other lessons have been politely declined. The willingness to be changed by the wall is what eventually moves through it.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Yi Jing 易經·繫辭下 (Xì Cí II, Commentary on Appended Judgments)

Read the full reading
461

臥wò薪xīn嘗cháng膽dǎn

wò xīn cháng dǎn

sleep on brushwood, taste gall

Endure self-imposed hardship and never let comfort dull your resolve, so you can rise from defeat.

You laid a bed of firewood under your own back and hung something bitter where you would taste it each morning, because comfort has a way of quietly talking you out of what you swore to do. Keep the sting close; it is the only alarm loyalty ever answers to.

成語 Adage Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source 《史記·越王句踐世家》 (Records of the Grand Historian); phrase crystallized in Song-era writing

Read the full reading
462

千qiān磨mó萬wàn擊jī還hái堅jiān勁jìng，任rèn爾ěr東dōng西xī南nán北běi風fēng

qiān mó wàn jī hái jiān jìng, rèn ěr dōng xī nán běi fēng

a thousand grindings, ten thousand blows, still firm and strong — let the winds blow east, west, south, north

Rooted resolve outlasts every battering; once your footing is real, no direction of pressure can topple you.

The bamboo bit down on the mountain and drove its root into cracked rock, and now the wind can come from anywhere it likes. Sink your grip where nothing soft grows, and let them push from all four sides — a thing that is truly rooted only answers by staying.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 木 Wood 馬 Year of the Horse

Source 《竹石》 by 鄭燮 (Zheng Xie / Zheng Banqiao), Qing dynasty

Read the full reading
463

山shān重chóng水shuǐ複fù疑yí無wú路lù，柳liǔ暗àn花huā明míng又yòu一yī村cūn

shān chóng shuǐ fù yí wú lù liǔ àn huā míng yòu yī cūn

mountains overlap, waters repeat, seeming no road; willows darkened, flowers bright, and there is another village

When all paths seem blocked, a new way suddenly opens; darkness before a breakthrough is not the end.

You stop because the path has folded back on itself three times and you cannot see what is ahead. But the village is there. It has always been there. The willows were only covering it, and you were only not close enough yet.

詩詞 Verse Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 木 Wood 馬 Year of the Horse

Source Lu You 陸游 (Song dynasty), 游山西村 'Touring the Mountain West Village'

Read the full reading
464

亡wáng羊yáng補bǔ牢láo

wáng yáng bǔ láo

the sheep is lost, then mend the fold

It is never too late to repair the damage after a loss.

You keep counting what already walked out through the gap, as if the arithmetic could undo it. The one sheep is gone. But the fence is still yours to mend tonight, and mending it is not an admission of failure, it is how the rest of the flock stays.

成語 Adage Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Strategies of the Warring States 戰國策

Read the full reading
465

吃chī一yī塹qiàn，長zhǎng一yī智zhì

chī yī qiàn zhǎng yī zhì

eat one pitfall, grow one wisdom

Learn from every setback; fall into a pit and gain a lesson.

The pit is the teaching; the falling is the enrollment. No one chooses the curriculum of failure, but those who complete the course come out with something that could not have been acquired from above ground. Every setback is a deposit in the account of knowing.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Looks back 土 Earth 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese proverb (yanyu, common in folk and literary contexts)

Read the full reading
466

留liú得dé青qīng山shān在zài，不bù怕pà沒méi柴chái燒shāo

liú dé qīng shān zài, bù pà méi chái shāo

keep the green hills, and you'll never fear having no firewood

As long as you protect the foundation — life, health, the source itself — everything lost can be regrown.

When the harvest fails, you do not burn the mountain for one last warm night. Guard the slope that keeps growing back, because a body and a life are the green hill, and firewood is only ever the thing they will make again.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Looks ahead 木 Wood 羊 Year of the Goat

Source folk proverb; recorded in Ming novel 《初刻拍案驚奇》

Read the full reading
467

福fú無wú雙shuāng至zhì，禍huò不bù單dān行xíng

fú wú shuāng zhì huò bù dān xíng

fortune does not come doubly, misfortune does not travel alone

Good fortune rarely doubles, but misfortune often comes in groups.

The string of difficulties is not a punishment but a pattern, one that knows its own end even when we cannot see it. Understanding that trouble runs in clusters keeps you from believing the cluster is the whole story. The weather breaks, always, and the single clear day is not far behind.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 水 Water 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in theatrical and literary usage)

Read the full reading
468

烈liè火huǒ見jiàn真zhēn金jīn

liè huǒ jiàn zhēn jīn

fierce fire reveals true gold

Adversity reveals true character; extreme difficulty shows who is genuine.

Gold submits to the fire and the fire cannot change what it is. Only the impurities leave; the gold itself remains, cleaner for the burning. What is tested by difficulty and survives that testing is not merely intact but clarified, stripped of everything that was not essential.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 火 Fire 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Traditional Chinese proverb (yanyu, common in folk literature)

Read the full reading
469

患huàn難nán見jiàn人rén心xīn

huàn nán jiàn rén xīn

hardship reveals the human heart

Adversity reveals a person's true character and intentions.

The comfortable season shows you everyone's face but not their heart. What a person is when everything is easy is preliminary information. The storm is the examination, and hardship is the only invigilator honest enough to hold the room while the truth is written down.

諺語 Proverb Adversity & Resilience Present-minded 火 Fire 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
470

春chūn江jiāng水shuǐ暖nuǎn鴨yā先xiān知zhī

chūn jiāng shuǐ nuǎn yā xiān zhī

spring river water warms, duck knows first

Those closest to a situation are the first to notice changes; those in the water feel the warmth before anyone watching from shore.

The duck is already there when the first warmth arrives, and this is not wisdom but position. Knowledge and proximity are two different things but in this moment they are the same: the one who is in the water knows something the one on the bank does not, not because of superior perception but because of where they are standing. Go where the information lives.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 水 Water 鼠 Year of the Rat

Source Su Shi 蘇軾·《惠崇春江晚景》 (Huì Chóng Chūn Jiāng Wǎn Jǐng, Song Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
471

一yī年nián之zhī計jì在zài於yú春chūn

yī nián zhī jì zài yú chūn

a year's plan lies in spring

Spring is the season for planning the whole year; seize the beginning to shape what follows.

Spring is not the first month but the first permission, the season in which the earth itself is participating in the idea of beginning again. The plan made when the seeds are chosen and the ground is turned carries the freshness of the season and the clarity of not-yet-having-started. This is the moment that shapes the rest.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Northern Qi Dynasty 北齊·顏之推《顏氏家訓》 (Yán Shì Jiā Xùn)

Read the full reading
472

春chūn有yǒu百bǎi花huā秋qiū有yǒu月yuè，夏xià有yǒu涼liáng風fēng冬dōng有yǒu雪xuě

chūn yǒu bǎi huā qiū yǒu yuè xià yǒu liáng fēng dōng yǒu xuě

spring has hundred flowers, autumn has moon, summer has cool breeze, winter has snow

Each season has its own beauty; if the mind is untroubled, every time of year is good.

Spring flower, autumn moon, summer wind, winter snow — the complaint about the season is always slightly wrong because each season holds its own particular gift and the grief is only in the comparison to another season's gift. The mind that is free enough to receive what each time of year actually offers finds nothing unacceptable. Every season is the right season for what it is.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Wumen Huikai 無門慧開·《無門關》 (Wú Mén Guān, Song Dynasty Chan text)

Read the full reading
473

春chūn眠mián不bù覺jué曉xiǎo

chūn mián bù jué xiǎo

spring sleep unaware of dawn

In spring, one sleeps deeply and doesn't notice the dawn; the body's alignment with seasonal rest.

In spring the body knows something the alarm clock does not: the season for deep rest is still present, and the dawn that arrives early is not a command but an invitation that may be declined. Sleep unaware of the hour is the body's vote for the natural over the scheduled, and sometimes that vote is the wisest one cast all week.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Meng Haoran 孟浩然·《春曉》 (Chūn Xiǎo, Spring Dawn, Tang Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
474

月yuè到dào中zhōng秋qiū分fèn外wài明míng

yuè dào zhōng qiū fèn wài míng

moon at mid-autumn exceptionally bright

The moon at Mid-Autumn Festival is exceptionally bright; beauty has its appointed peaks.

The moon is always there but the mid-autumn moon has an audience and a quality of collective attention that makes it brighter than all the other moons of the year. What is beautiful is made more beautiful by being recognized together, by the shared gaze that turns a thing's ordinary light into something the whole world lifts its face toward.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Traditional Chinese saying (common at Mid-Autumn Festival)

Read the full reading
475

月yuè有yǒu陰yīn晴qíng圓yuán缺quē

yuè yǒu yīn qíng yuán quē

the moon has its shadow and light, its fullness and its waning

Like human grief and joy, the moon waxes and wanes; imperfection is the natural order.

You have been quietly ashamed that you cannot hold onto a full feeling, that joy keeps thinning back toward dark. The moon has never once managed it either, and no one calls the moon a failure. What empties in you tonight is only rounding toward the next full face.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 水 Water 兔 Year of the Rabbit

Source Su Shi 蘇軾, 水調歌頭

Read the full reading
476

仁rén者zhě樂lè山shān，智zhì者zhě樂lè水shuǐ

rén zhě lè shān zhì zhě lè shuǐ

benevolent person delights in mountains, wise person delights in water

The virtuous love mountains for their steadiness; the wise love water for its adaptability.

The mountain stands without moving and the river runs without stopping and both are forms of the persistent. What you are drawn to in nature is also what you are drawn toward in yourself: the virtue that wants to be as solid as the mountain, the intelligence that wants to be as flexible as the river. Nature is also a mirror, showing us who we aspire to be.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Analects of Confucius 論語·雍也 (Yōng Yě VI)

Read the full reading
477

健jiàn康kāng如rú山shān

jiàn kāng rú shān

health like a mountain

May your health be as enduring as a mountain; wishing robust and lasting health.

The mountain does not ask for rest; it simply continues. What is solidly healthy has a quality of presence, a density of being, that the merely not-sick does not share. To cultivate health is to build a mountain inside the self, one that will hold against weather and time.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Looks ahead 土 Earth 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Traditional Chinese blessing (common in folk and festive contexts)

Read the full reading
478

綠lǜ水shuǐ青qīng山shān就jiù是shì金jīn山shān銀yín山shān

lǜ shuǐ qīng shān jiù shì jīn shān yín shān

green water and green mountains are the mountains of gold and silver

A clean environment is true wealth; nature well cared for is worth more than gold.

The mountain that keeps its green and the river that keeps its clarity are producing something the gold mine cannot: the conditions for all other life. Wealth extracted from a mountain that is left gray and the water left dark is wealth that has spent itself completely on the getting. The mountain kept green is the one that keeps giving.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 龍 Year of the Dragon

Source Traditional Chinese ecological wisdom; contemporary environmental philosophy

Read the full reading
479

吐tǔ故gù納nà新xīn

tǔ gù nà xīn

exhale the old, inhale the new

Discard the old and take in the new; renewal through releasing the outdated.

The lungs know something the mind sometimes forgets: that holding on is the beginning of suffocation. Every breath is a small ceremony of release and reception, and the body performs it without being asked. What the breath does for the body, discernment can do for a life.

成語 Adage Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Zhuangzi 莊子·刻意 (Kè Yì chapter)

Read the full reading
480

日rì月yuè如rú梭suō，光guāng陰yīn似sì箭jiàn

rì yuè rú suō guāng yīn sì jiàn

sun and moon like a shuttle, time like an arrow

Time flies; the sun and moon pass like a weaver's shuttle, time moves as fast as an arrow.

The shuttle crosses the loom before the eye completes its tracking, and the year does the same. This is not to induce panic but clarity: the very fact that time moves this way is information about how to hold it. With both hands, fully present, without the distraction of thinking about holding it better some other time.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying; common in educational and literary contexts

Read the full reading
481

病bìng來lái如rú山shān倒dǎo，病bìng去qù如rú抽chōu絲sī

bìng lái rú shān dǎo bìng qù rú chōu sī

illness comes like a mountain falling, illness leaves like pulling silk

Illness strikes suddenly but heals slowly; recovery takes patience.

The mountain falls with the decisiveness of one enormous event, and the silk unravels slowly, thread by thread, and there is no way to hurry either. What illness takes from the body in one catastrophic arrival the body reclaims over the long, measured time of the silk. Patience with the slow recovery is the final act of the illness, and it is the one most likely to be skipped.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 土 Earth 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Traditional Chinese folk health proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
482

落luò花huā流liú水shuǐ

luò huā liú shuǐ

fallen petals, flowing water

Things in a state of utter defeat or disintegration; beauty departing without return.

The petal that has left the branch does not return to it, and the current that carries it does not circle back. Some departures are total and some endings are not followed by beginnings. The willingness to name a thing finished, even a beautiful thing, is its own form of dignity.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Looks back 水 Water 蛇 Year of the Snake

Source Tang Dynasty 唐·李煜詞 (Lǐ Yù Cí, Li Yu lyric poetry)

Read the full reading
483

人rén老lǎo心xīn不bù老lǎo

rén lǎo xīn bù lǎo

person old, heart not old

The body may age, but the spirit remains young; youthful heart regardless of age.

The body accumulates its years honestly and without negotiation, but the heart has a different contract with time. What stays curious, what keeps finding things delightful, what can still be moved by music or a good idea — this is the part that refuses the full agreement with age. Keep it fed. The body's autumn is not the heart's.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 火 Fire 馬 Year of the Horse

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying

Read the full reading
484

老lǎo當dāng益yì壯zhuàng

lǎo dāng yì zhuàng

old yet increasingly vigorous

The elderly should be even more spirited and strong; remain vigorous and ambitious in old age.

There is a certain kind of vigor that only age can produce, the product of all the failures survived and all the lessons absorbed and all the complications that were once daunting and are now simply problems with known solutions. The old who are increasing in vitality are not fighting their age but riding it, using the momentum of a life well lived. Old and increasing.

成語 Adage Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 火 Fire 馬 Year of the Horse

Source Hou Han Shu 後漢書·馬援傳 (Mǎ Yuán biography)

Read the full reading
485

少shào年nián不bù識shí愁chóu滋zī味wèi

shào nián bù shí chóu zī wèi

young person does not recognize the taste of sorrow

Youth doesn't know the full weight of life's sorrows; experience brings true understanding.

The young person's sadness is real but has not yet been aged by the knowledge of what will follow. The older sorrow is flavored by everything that came before it, and the comparison changes the texture. This is not a loss but an accumulation: what youth has in freshness, experience has in depth, and both are forms of something genuine.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Looks back 水 Water 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Xin Qiji 辛棄疾·《醜奴兒》 (Chǒu Nú Ér, Song Dynasty ci poem)

Read the full reading
486

山shān中zhōng歲suì月yuè長cháng

shān zhōng suì yuè cháng

in the mountain years and months are long

In stillness and nature, time moves differently; the peaceful life feels full and unhurried.

Time in the mountain moves on a different scale than time in the city, and the person who has spent a season in the high quiet comes back knowing something about duration that they did not know before. Stillness does not slow time; it allows time to become fully itself, without being rushed past before it can be inhabited.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in hermit and Taoist literature)

Read the full reading
487

年nián年nián歲suì歲suì花huā相xiāng似sì，歲suì歲suì年nián年nián人rén不bù同tóng

nián nián suì suì huā xiāng sì suì suì nián nián rén bù tóng

year after year the flowers are similar, year after year the people are different

Nature remains constant while people change; the flower returns, the person does not.

The flower comes back; the person is changed by the interval or simply does not come back at all. Nature has this form of consistency that makes it the background against which human change is most legible. The same blossoms on the same branch in the same garden frame a different person looking at them each year, and the garden does not notice.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Liu Xiyi 劉希夷·《代悲白頭翁》 (Dài Bēi Bái Tóu Wēng, Tang Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
488

心xīn靜jìng自zì然rán涼liáng

xīn jìng zì rán liáng

heart still, naturally cool

When the mind is calm, the body feels cool; inner peace creates physical comfort.

The summer that cannot be negotiated away from the thermometer can be handled differently by the interior climate of the person inside it. The still heart does not add its own heat to the ambient heat; it finds coolness in not struggling against the warmth. This is not self-deception but a genuine form of comfort available to those who do not fight the season.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 水 Water 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in health and Taoist contexts)

Read the full reading
489

花huā無wú百bǎi日rì紅hóng

huā wú bǎi rì hóng

flower has no hundred days red

Nothing beautiful or good lasts forever; enjoy what you have while it lasts.

The peony at full bloom does not argue against the coming of autumn, nor does it bloom twice to make up for what was missed. It is entirely what it is for exactly as long as it is that, and then the petals go where petals go. There is no shame in being temporary when you are, for a moment, magnificent.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Traditional Chinese folk proverb (yanyu)

Read the full reading
490

落luò日rì無wú限xiàn好hǎo，只zhǐ是shì近jìn黃huáng昏hūn

luò rì wú xiàn hǎo zhǐ shì jìn huáng hūn

setting sun infinitely beautiful, only that it approaches dusk

The sunset is infinitely beautiful, but it is already near dusk — beauty tinged with the knowledge of ending.

The light that comes at the end of the day is the most saturated the sky will produce, burning orange and deep red at the very moment it is leaving. Knowing that something beautiful is near its end is not a reason to look away; it is the reason the looking has a particular quality of attention. The ending is part of the light.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 火 Fire 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Li Shangyin 李商隱·《登樂遊原》 (Dēng Lè Yóu Yuán, Tang Dynasty poem)

Read the full reading
491

過guò眼yǎn雲yún煙yān

guò yǎn yún yān

passing before the eyes like clouds and smoke

Transient as passing clouds; things that quickly pass before the eyes without leaving a trace.

The cloud passes across the eye's field and the smoke thins and both are gone, and this is not loss but the nature of the transient. Not everything that appears is meant to stay, and the eye that grasps at every passing cloud is never still enough to see clearly what is actually here. Some things are for looking at, not holding.

成語 Adage Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 羊 Year of the Goat

Source Su Shi 蘇軾 derived; common Chinese classical expression

Read the full reading
492

一yī笑xiào解jiě百bǎi愁chóu

yī xiào jiě bǎi chóu

one smile dissolves a hundred sorrows

A single smile can dissolve countless worries; laughter is the best medicine.

The body does not know the difference between a fully earned smile and one chosen from within the sorrow, and what the face does, the interior tends to follow. The single genuine smile is not a denial of the hundred worries but a declaration that the person smiling is larger than any of them. This is what joy at the center of difficulty looks like.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 火 Fire 猴 Year of the Monkey

Source Traditional Chinese folk saying (common in health and social wisdom)

Read the full reading
493

天tiān下xià無wú不bù散sàn之zhī筵yán席xí

tiān xià wú bù sàn zhī yán xí

under heaven there is no banquet that does not break up

Every gathering, however warm, must eventually end.

You are already grieving the end of something you are still inside of, and that grief is the receipt for how good it is. Nothing at this table was meant to last, which is precisely why you should taste it now. The lamps going out later is not a betrayal, it is the shape of an evening.

俗語 Saying Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source 醒世恆言

Read the full reading
494

日rì出chū而ér作zuò，日rì入rù而ér息xī

rì chū ér zuò rì rù ér xī

sun rises work, sun sets rest

Rise with the sun to work, rest when it sets; live in accord with the natural rhythms.

The body does not need convincing when it is aligned with the light. Work begun at dawn carries the energy of the rising world; rest welcomed at dusk allows the night to do its quiet restoration. Those who follow this cadence rarely complain of exhaustion, for the day itself is carrying them.

詩詞 Verse Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Shi Jing 詩經 / ancient folk song 《擊壤歌》 (Jī Rǎng Gē, Striking the Clod Song)

Read the full reading
495

早zǎo睡shuì早zǎo起qǐ身shēn體tǐ好hǎo

zǎo shuì zǎo qǐ shēn tǐ hǎo

sleep early, rise early, the body is well

Early to bed and early to rise promotes good health; regular rhythms sustain the body.

The body runs on light. It was built to wake with the sun and rest when it sets, and every time you fight that rhythm you borrow against a debt the body will eventually collect. Sleep early, rise with the world, and the world meets you before it has used up its best hours.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Chinese folk proverb 民間諺語; Benjamin Franklin's phrase has a Chinese parallel

Read the full reading
496

鶴hè髮fà童tóng顏yán

hè fà tóng yán

crane hair, child face

White hair but youthful face; look young and healthy despite old age; vitality in aging.

The crane is associated with the long life and also with a certain lightness, the quality of moving through the world without excessive weight. The face that has stayed young alongside white hair has managed to keep whatever youth keeps: not its ignorance but its freshness, the capacity to be surprised and delighted by what is in front of it.

成語 Adage Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 水 Water 雞 Year of the Rooster

Source Traditional Chinese saying (common in auspicious and longevity contexts)

Read the full reading
497

天tiān高gāo雲yún淡dàn

tiān gāo yún dàn

sky high, clouds thin

Clear autumn skies with high, thin clouds; a feeling of spaciousness and serenity.

The sky of autumn does not fill itself up with the heavy clouds of summer. It stretches without obstruction to its highest pitch, and the few clouds there are drift at the altitude of things that have arrived and are not worried about where they are going. Some days the world itself demonstrates what serenity looks like, and today is one of those days.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 狗 Year of the Dog

Source Traditional Chinese seasonal expression (common in poetry and folk culture)

Read the full reading
498

冬dōng吃chī蘿luó蔔bo夏xià吃chī薑jiāng不bù勞láo醫yī生shēng開kāi藥yào方fāng

dōng chī luó bo xià chī jiāng bù láo yī shēng kāi yào fāng

winter eat turnip summer eat ginger, no need for doctor to write prescription

Eating turnip in winter and ginger in summer keeps the doctor away; follow seasonal foods for good health.

The garden knows the season and offers accordingly, and the body that accepts the offer stays in alignment with the turning year. Turnip in winter warms from inside; ginger in summer disperses the humid heat. This is medicine before it needed a name, available to anyone who watches what grows when.

俗語 Saying Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 木 Wood 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Traditional Chinese folk health proverb

Read the full reading
499

心xīn寬kuān體tǐ胖pàng

xīn kuān tǐ pàng

heart wide, body round

A generous and unworried heart leads to a comfortable and healthy body.

The person who is not contracting against the world's insults and surprises does not carry that contraction in their body. What the heart does with its grudges shows eventually in the shoulders and the stomach and the quality of sleep. The wide heart is not naive but capacious, able to hold what comes without gripping it, and the body knows the difference.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 土 Earth 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Traditional Chinese folk health proverb

Read the full reading
500

禍huò從cóng口kǒu出chū，病bìng從cóng口kǒu入rù

huò cóng kǒu chū bìng cóng kǒu rù

disaster exits from mouth, illness enters through mouth

Reckless words invite disaster; careless eating invites illness — the mouth is a two-way gate.

The mouth is the most consequential opening on the body, managing both what enters and what departs. Words sent outward and food taken inward are both subject to the same question: is this going to help or harm. The thoughtful person pauses at the gate, in both directions.

諺語 Proverb Nature, Seasons & Health Present-minded 金 Metal 豬 Year of the Pig

Source Traditional Chinese health and wisdom saying (common in both contexts)

Read the full reading

No proverb matches that. Loosen a filter, or clear them all.

About the library

Are these real Chinese proverbs?

Yes. Every proverb here is drawn from a named classical source, such as the Analects, the Tao Te Ching, Mencius, the Art of War, or the dynastic histories, or from attested folk tradition. Sayings that are only English calques translated into Chinese, like the false “still waters run deep,” are deliberately left out.

What is the difference between an adage, a proverb, and a saying?

Chinese sorts its wisdom by form. 成語 (chéngyǔ) are four-character classical idioms, often carrying a whole story. 諺語 (yànyǔ) are folk proverbs. 俗語 (súyǔ) are colloquial sayings. A few entries are 詩詞, lines of classical verse. You can filter the library by each type.

How are the proverbs tied to the zodiac animals?

The pairing of a proverb with a Chinese zodiac year is our own editorial reading, meant for reflection, not traditional canon. It is a way to let your animal lead you to a line worth keeping, never a claim that the tradition assigns it.

Can I hear how each proverb is pronounced?

Yes. Press the speaker on any proverb, or on the one you draw from the pond, to hear it read aloud in Mandarin by your browser's voice. The per-character pinyin sits above every character so you can follow the sound and the tones.

Keep wandering

These proverbs sit behind the whole tradition. See the years they belong to in the Chinese zodiac, the signs they cross in the Western zodiac, or how place shapes fortune in Feng Shui. Or find your Zodi Animal and let it lead you to a line worth keeping.

Ready to make them yours? Walk the Path of Mastery, then see what you have gathered in your collection.

The Primal Oracle

These proverbs are living Chinese cultural heritage, presented with per-character pinyin and their classical sources out of respect for the tradition. Meanings are given faithfully; the pairing of a proverb to a Chinese zodiac year is our own reading for reflection, not tradition, and nothing here is medical, psychological, financial, or legal advice.

守shǒu得dé雲yún開kāi見jiàn月yuè明míng

Hold on until the clouds part, and the bright moon appears.
